data.source: Online participant panels (Amazon Mechanical Turk, Survey Sampling International) and university student pools
data.wave_or_subset: Eight separate experiments (Studies 1a, 1b, 2a, 2b, 3, 4, 5) conducted between 2011 and 2016
data.sample_size: Varied by study (e.g., 119 in Study 1a; 405 in Study 1b; 149 in Study 2a; 127 in Study 2b; 425 in Study 4; 108 in Study 5)
data.access_details: Not publicly stated; data collected by authors and not indicated as openly available
data.notes: Most data collected via Amazon Mechanical Turk; some via university subject pools. Multiple independent samples across studies.

method.description: Across eight experiments, participants were exposed to product rating distributions (bimodal vs. unimodal or high-variance) and asked to evaluate products on perceived self-expressiveness, desirability, and willingness to pay while manipulating factors such as self-concept clarity, consumption context, rated attribute, and identity focus.
steps: Recruit participants for each experiment (MTurk, SSI, or university pool).
Randomly assign participants to conditions manipulating rating distribution shape and contextual/individual moderators (e.g., self-concept clarity threat, consumption context, attribute type).
Present product information with simulated rating histograms holding mean ratings constant but varying distribution (bimodal vs. unimodal).
Collect outcome measures: perceived self-expressiveness, desirability ratings, willingness to pay, preference choice scales.
Administer manipulation checks (e.g., Self-Concept Clarity Scale) and demographic items.
Analyze data using regressions, ANOVAs, and moderated-mediation models to test interactions and indirect effects.
method.models: Linear regression, ANOVA, moderated mediation (e.g., PROCESS models with indirect effects and interaction terms)
method.outcome_variable: Primary: product desirability rating; Secondary: willingness to pay; perceived self-expressiveness; preference choice scores
method.independent_variables: Rating distribution (bimodal vs. unimodal); self-concept clarity (low/conflicted vs. high/clear); consumption context (self-expressive vs. functional); rated attribute (style vs. quality); identity focus (individual vs. group)
method.control_variables: Rating means held constant; demographic variables occasionally included (not specified in summary)

results.summary: Across the eight experiments, products with bimodal (polarizing) rating distributions were perceived as more self-expressive than those with unimodal distributions. Individuals with temporarily or chronically low self-concept clarity, and those in self-expressive contexts, rated polarizing products as more desirable and were willing to pay more for them. These effects were mediated by perceived self-expressiveness and were strongest when the rated attribute was self-expressive (e.g., style) and when participants sought to express individual rather than group identity.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Desirability interaction coefficient (Self-concept clarity × Rating distribution, Study 2a)
Three-way interaction on WTP (Context × Attribute × Distribution, Study 4)
